title: Sts. Abraham, Isaac & Jacob the Patriarchs — Sts. Cyrus & John (Relics) & St. Pappias the Martyr
narrative:
The Holy Patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob the Fathers of the Faith, the Translation of the Relics of the Holy Unmercenary Martyrs Cyrus and John, and St. Pappias the Martyr

On the twenty-eighth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the three holy Patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — the three fathers of Israel whose names God declared to Moses from the burning bush as the formula of divine self-identification: 'I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' In this divine declaration, the three patriarchs are not merely historical ancestors but the living companions of the living God, the men with whom God entered into covenant and in whose names God continues to identify himself across all subsequent generations. Jesus himself used this formula in his argument with the Sadducees against the denial of resurrection: 'He is not the God of the dead but of the living,' meaning that Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, with whom God continues to identify himself, must therefore continue to live — a reading of the Exodus text that the tradition has always regarded as one of the most luminous moments of biblical exegesis in the entire Gospel narrative.

The theology of the three Patriarchs in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the covenant across the generations: Abraham received the promise, Isaac carried it, and Jacob transmitted it, and together they constitute the first three links in the chain of faithful transmission that runs from the call of Abraham in Ur of the Chaldees through the entire history of Israel and the Church to the present day. The Ethiopian tradition holds a particular reverence for Abraham, who is among the most frequently invoked of the Old Testament figures in the liturgy, partly because of the universal significance of his faith — 'Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness' is the Pauline foundation of the theology of grace — and partly because of the proximity of Ethiopia to the geographical and historical world of the ancient Semitic cultures from which the patriarchal traditions emerged. The feast of the three Patriarchs near the end of Sene is the month's great Old Testament anchor, the moment at which the community of the New Covenant remembers the roots from which it grows.

The Translation of the Relics of Saints Cyrus and John — the great unmercenary physician-martyrs of Alexandria — brings to this feast the beloved healers whose holy remains were moved from their resting place to a more honoured location in one of the liturgical acts of translation that the tradition regards as a form of ongoing glorification: the Church declares the holiness of its martyrs not only on their feast days but in the physical care it takes of their bodies, in the solemn processions that carry their relics from place to place, in the basilicas and shrines built to house them. Cyrus and John are among the most celebrated of the physician-saints of the Egyptian tradition: a doctor and a soldier who were martyred under Diocletian in Alexandria and whose relics, brought to Menouthis near Alexandria, became the centre of a great healing shrine that drew pilgrims from across the Christian world. Their translation on this day is the tradition's celebration of the living power of the dead: the bodies of the martyrs continue to heal because the martyrs themselves continue to intercede, and the translation of the relics is the Church's formal recognition that the healing ministry of Cyrus and John has not ended with their deaths but been extended and intensified by their glorification.

Saint Pappias the Martyr completes the assembly of this rich twenty-eighth day of Sene as the tradition's witness that the covenant of the Patriarchs, made with Abraham and renewed with Isaac and Jacob and fulfilled in Christ, continues to require faithful witnesses in every generation who are willing to maintain it at the cost of their lives. Pappias died for the same faith that Abraham believed and Isaac and Jacob transmitted and Cyrus and John sealed with their blood, and his name in the Church's calendar is the tradition's insistence that this chain of faithfulness is not a chain of the famous and the extraordinary but a chain that includes also the ordinary, the unremembered, and the barely recorded, whose links are as essential to the whole as any of the great shining links that theology and hagiography have most polished. The twenty-eighth day of Sene gathers the first fathers of the faith, the healing martyrs of Alexandria, and the unnamed faithful who kept the chain unbroken. Amen.
